turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning, first this term, in Case 13簫1067, OBB Personenverkehr v. Sachs. Mr. Basombrio.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This personal injury action is based upon an accident that occurred in Austria. If this Court agrees, there's no need to reach the other question related to agency, so I will start first with the based簫upon issue. In Nelson, this Court set forth a framework to analyze the based簫upon question. And this Court held that courts must begin their analysis by identifying the particular conduct on which the action is based. The decision uses words such as basis, foundation, and gravamen. So here 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But in 簫簫 in that case, there was a distinction between State activity 簫簫 that is, police activity 簫簫 and the commercial activity in hiring the plaintiff. Here, I think it's conceded that the activity 簫簫 running a railroad 簫簫 that's commercial. So we don't have the commercial/State action division.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that's correct. Here, there are two alleged commercial activities. One of them is the sale of the ticket in the United States, and the other one are the acts and omissions that resulted in the accident in Austria. So what I would suggest is that we look at the complaint and see what it is that the plaintiff has alleged.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Just as a matter of background, suppose a hypothetical case 簫簫 no foreign sovereign. It was a private corporation in Austria. Would there then be jurisdiction under the Due Process Clause in your view? You don't have to prevail on 簫簫 on that issue, but as a background issue, what 簫簫 do you have a position?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Our position is that if OBB was a private entity, there would not be jurisdiction over OBB. There would not be general jurisdiction after this Court holding in Daimler and there would also 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: And Daimler is your best case for that proposition?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Do 簫簫 do you think that it's the same test? In other words, is the test a specific jurisdiction test here, based簫on, arising簫from, that we are basically asking the same question as we would be asking if this were a 簫簫 a foreign corporation?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor. Let me explain, if I may, why not. Congress could have chosen that wording in the personal jurisdiction analysis in the case law, but they decided on another phrase, based簫upon, instead. So I believe that what this Court needs to do is give some guidance 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but why 簫簫 I mean, it doesn't seem to me that that wording is very different from the wording that we've used in specific jurisdiction cases. The wording here is "based on" 簫簫 we've used "arising out of." Sometimes we've used "related to." In some respects 簫簫 I mean, it's pretty clear that the FSIA is meant to ensure that when a foreign government is acting as a commercial actor, it gets treated like a foreign corporation. And the language here is very similar, right? There's the insistence on a sufficient contact, a minimum contact, and then there is the insistence on a particular kind of relationship between that contact 簫簫 contact and the claim. So it seems 簫簫 I guess the question is: Why should we think of these two questions as at all different? And I don't know, by the way, I mean, that it would hurt you if they were the same, because it might just be there would be no specific jurisdiction here, you know? But I guess I'm having trouble of thinking why it is that there 簫簫 that there would be a different test.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: The reason why is because the FSIA takes place of the 簫簫 both the subject matter jurisdiction analysis and the personal jurisdiction analysis. Both are combined into one test. So although I would agree certainly that some aspects of the personal jurisdictional analysis are part of that test, it goes beyond that. It also goes into subject matter jurisdiction which is a different set of policy determinations that Congress made that are in some way overlapping of the personal jurisdiction questions, but I don't think they're completely aligned.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm 簫簫 I'm sorry. I'm 簫簫 I don't even understand why we're talking about based簫upon. As Justice Ginsburg said, there's no dispute here that whether the based簫upon is the ticket sale or the operation of the train, both of them are commercial activities. Isn't the work in substantial contact with the United States? Isn't that what we should be looking at instead? Was this commercial activity substantial enough? The operation of the train and the ticket sale here, did it have a substantial contact with the United States?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: There's a three簫step analysis, Your Honor, in Nelson. First, you have to identify the particular conduct, the actions, not the causes of action, which is what the en banc court did. They focused on the legal claims. This Court said you have to focus on the acts. Here, the acts 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So we did that in the context of deciding when something was a sovereign act as opposed to a commercial act. We know this is a commercial act. So 簫簫 and we 簫簫 I'm just confused. Why isn't the work 簫簫 why shouldn't the work be done by substantial contact with the U.S.?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: It's a three簫step analysis. First, you identify the activity. Secondly, you decide whether it is commercial or not.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Whether it's 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: And third, whether there's substantial contact.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 commercial or sovereign.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: So there are three steps. What I'm saying is that the Ninth Circuit erred. They didn't look at the conduct first. They looked at the legal claims. If you look at the 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Even if they had looked at the conduct, it's commercial.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: It's commercial. But if you look at the conduct and you identify it as the accident in Austria 簫簫 which is what the plaintiff claimed. In JA 15, paragraphs 3 to 8, they alleged that there was an unsafe boarding platform, a gap at the platform, et cetera, et cetera. All of these things happened in Austria 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: But how do you propose 簫簫 how do you propose that we determine whether it's based on commercial activity? Take their failure簫to簫warn claim. Why isn't that based on something that occurred in the United States? You're just 簫簫 are you just asking us to 簫簫 to step back and say, well, in that case we 簫簫 we really think they're just trying 簫簫 they have a 簫簫 they have a tort that occurred in Austria, a negligence tort that occurred in Austria, and they're just trying to plead around it with these other claims?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well, there's no allegation in the Complaint that the failure to warn was something that happened in the United States. It's found in the same set of paragraphs, 3 through 8, that relate to the acts and omissions in Austria. It also would make no sense to argue that there was a failure to warn in the United States because that would mean that the Eurail Pass itself would have to warn about all conditions at hundreds of potential railroad stations in Europe.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, it might be 簫簫 it might be a claim that couldn't prevail, but why does that answer the question?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well, the failure to warn that's alleged relates to the facts that arise in Austria, and, therefore, the cause of action arises in Austria because that's where the acts or omissions occurred if 簫簫 if one looks at what's alleged in the Complaint itself.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Would 簫簫 would you agree and 簫簫 and, as you've been doing, take out the agency question 簫簫 but would you agree if 簫簫 if what had happened here was that the ticket was not honored. You know, the plaintiff bought a ticket, and the ticket was not honored, and 簫簫 and the suit was where the ticket was purchased, would you agree that that's based簫on?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: If this was a breach of contract case 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Just a breach of contract case.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: 簫簫 and the breach 簫簫 the allegation of the breach was that when the Respondent showed up they did not honor the ticket, that would be based upon an activity in Austria because it's in Austria that that ticket got honored.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: In Austria, even though she bought the ticket in the United States? You think even the breach of contract case could not be brought in the United States?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Correct, because the 簫簫 the wrongful act, the breach, arises in Austria.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What about 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose we disagree with that 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose we disagree with that answer? Do you lose the case?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose we disagree with the answer that you gave to Justice Kagan. Suppose we disagree that that suit has to be in Austria. Can you still prevail on the facts of this case? The hypothetical was the ticket is not honored. Suppose we disagree with your answer. Can you still prevail on the facts that it 簫簫 that it did, in fact, occur in this case?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, but I would still prevail based on the agency argument that we have presented.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: On what 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But you 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Could you go back and explain to me what 簫簫 I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You 簫簫 you'd lose on 簫簫 on the other point.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You 簫簫 you acknowledge that you would lose on the other point if 簫簫 if that hypothetical came out the other way?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: No, I do not.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: I believe 簫簫 let me clarify, Your Honor, if I may. My answer to Justice 簫簫 Justice Kagan was that I believe that a breach of contract claim for dishonor of the ticket in Austria has to be brought in Austria.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Because 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Justice 簫簫 because that's where the breach occurred that gives rise to the cause of action. Justice Kennedy said to me, suppose that we disagree with you, can you still win the case? And my answer was, I could still win, I believe, under the agency alternative argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But not 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: What about apart 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 not under 簫簫 under the argument you're 簫簫 you're first making?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Not if the Court ruled against me, no. But I believe 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: On that 簫簫 why? Why? I mean, couldn't 簫簫 couldn't you make the argument that the question ought to be decided not 簫簫 not by where the 簫簫 where the contract was breached but where the contract was made? I mean, we could hold 簫簫 we could hold that, and that would not affect your case.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: That's true. I 簫簫 I do not believe that a breach of contract claim under the fact scenario that you provided, Your Honor, would give rise to a claim in the United States because the breach happened 簫簫 would have happened in Austria.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Counsel, I want to come back to Justice Kagan's speculation of 簫簫 of whether based簫on is 簫簫 is nothing more than due process. It seems to me that the definition of commercial activity carried on in the United States by a foreign state is the due process test. The definition is, "A commercial activity carried on in the United States by a foreign state means commercial activity carried on by such state and having substantial contact with the United States." That sounds to me like 簫簫 like the due process test. But what is required here is not just a commercial activity carried on in the United States, it has to be based on a commercial activity carried on in the United States. And it seems to me that is something 簫簫 something added to the 簫簫 to the constitutional test.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And that's why I started my discussion by referring to this holding 簫簫 the holding of this Court in Nelson which has to look at the particular conduct. The particular conduct at issue is not the sale of the ticket, it's the acts and omissions that resulted in the accident in Austria.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What 簫簫 what if there are acts or omissions in two 簫簫 let's say you have a flight from New York to Vienna. And in New York, someone negligently sets or whatever they do with the landing gear, okay? So then the plane takes off, and then in Vienna, because of the negligence in New York, it's a rough landing, somebody gets a concussion.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Uh簫huh.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Where is the gravamen of the action in that case? Can you bring that in 簫簫 in the United States?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Potentially, you could bring it in the United States. And the difference between your scenario and this case is that in that scenario, the service was provided, started in 簫簫 starting in the United States. There's contact with the United States because the foreign airline came here and conducted a commercial activity in the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Because it's 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: That's what different簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: 簫簫 a typical tort. If the negligence occurs in one place, and the impact is the other, you could bring the suit in either place, as far as our notions of personal jurisdictions, either injury in the state or conduct in the state causing injury outside it. Those are typical long簫arm bases of jurisdictions. So the Chief's hypothetical where the negligence occurred in the United States, that you agree would be a case that could be brought in the United States because the relevant conduct occurred there.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Correct, in that case 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, correct. I don't understand, then, what gravamen means in the Nelson decision. Gravamen means one place, right?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Or do you think you could have a lot of different 簫簫 I mean, if it's the gravamen of your complaint, I think you have to choose, don't you, one or the other?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well, I would 簫簫 I was 簫簫 I would understand your example, Chief Justice, to mean that the act that caused the injury was whatever they didn't do upon takeoff to the plane, or 簫簫 or 簫簫 as I understood your example, that cause of that injury was in the United States.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: So can you have more than one place under the based簫upon analysis in Nelson, or has you 簫簫 have you got to decide there's only one place where you could bring the action?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well, the airline example 簫簫 and this is something I thought about. It's somewhat a complicated example because we have conventions, international treaties that deal with 簫簫 with airline cases. I would imagine that, theoretically, there could be one more 簫簫 more than one location, but not on the facts of this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: In the Chief Justice's example, he 簫簫 he stated that there was negligence in the United States. But what if there's no evidence of that? There's a 簫簫 there's a problem with the landing gear when the plane lands in Austria, and the claim is that there was a failure to inspect in the United States, or a failure to do proper maintenance in the United States. Would that be different?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: That may be different. Again, because the transportation was provided from the United States geographically, there 簫簫 there is no doubt in that question that commercial act was carried on in the United States by the airline. And that's different here.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I suppose it's 簫簫 it's 簫簫 it's proper to say that where you have negligence that causes an injury, the 簫簫 the complaint is based on both. You 簫簫 you don't have liability without the negligence. You don't have liability without the injury. So why can't you say based簫on, in 簫簫 in that situation, would enable either one to 簫簫 to sustain the cause of action?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: In the airplane example, perhaps either jurisdiction. In this example, if you look at the Complaint 簫簫 and we're guided by the allegations of the Complaint, it is 簫簫 the acts are only alleged to have happened and the omissions to have happened 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Getting back to our earlier discussion, questions from me and from Justice Kagan, it seemed to me you have to say the due process analysis is insufficient under this statute because 簫簫 then fill in the blank. And Justice Scalia was asking you the same question.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: One 簫簫 one answer might be because otherwise there would be no necessity for a statute. Due process applies anyway. But the counterargument is, well, there has to be because the statute makes the distinction between sovereign and commercial and so it has a real purpose.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right. The due process analysis is incorporated into the statute, but it's not the only thing to think about. For example 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: You did answer, I think 簫簫 you did answer, rendering Justice Kennedy's question somewhat academic, that there would be no specific jurisdiction in the United States if all that happened here was a ticket purchase from an agent where the injurious conduct occurred abroad and the railroad operates solely abroad. You answered that whether it were the due process specific jurisdiction inquiry or the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act, the answer would be the same, that the ticket sale in the United States was not enough.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But, of course, my question was suppose that the due process analysis is sufficient to sustain jurisdiction. Then what? Then does based簫on still have a separate meaning as a separate requirement that has to be met and has not been met here? And if so, why?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: It has not been met here because from a general jurisdictional perspective 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But not general jurisdiction.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I was really talking about specific jurisdiction.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: And, of course, Justice Scalia is absolutely right with respect to general jurisdiction, that all you look at is the contacts. But with respect to specific jurisdiction 簫簫 this is a company that doesn't have pervasive contacts 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 it's critical to the due process analysis that there be a relationship, and a real relationship between the particular contact with the United States and the lawsuit in the exact same way that this statute focuses on. And so, again, I don't know if it hurts you. It might be, as Justice Ginsburg says, that there wouldn't be specific jurisdiction here for a corporation. But 簫簫 but 簫簫 but why shouldn't we treat those two things exactly the same way?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: There is 簫簫 there is 簫簫 if I may answer that two parts of your question. First, there is no specific jurisdiction if OBB were a private entity because the Respondent is not suing for breach of the ticket; it's not alleging that it was not honored. She's suing for something that happened in Austria. The specific acts happened outside the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Yes. Well, that's an answer for why there is no specific jurisdiction here.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: And you can make the exact same answer in the completely private context. And you 簫簫

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 could well be right. I'm want to ask Mr. Fisher about that.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But it's not an answer for why the tests should be different.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: The test 簫簫 the test 簫簫 my belief is that the test should incorporate due process analysis. But because we are also dealing with subject matter jurisdiction, which is a different analysis than personal jurisdiction, there are other policy considerations. The policy considerations decisions that Congress made were based on territory. If one want 簫簫 if one looks at the first, the second, and the third clause, they're all territory簫based distinctions. In 簫簫 in the case Amerada Hess, this Court held that tort actions are meant to be encompassed by the second section of the 簫簫 of the exception, the non 簫簫 noncommercial torts exception. That requires that the tort happened in the United States in order to be 簫簫 to have subject matter on personal jurisdiction. It wouldn't fit here. Justice Ginsburg referred to direct effects. That would come under the third clause, which deals with commercial activity outside of the U.S.; again, a territorial line, having an effect here. The first clause draws the territorial line here and says that commercial activity has to happen in this side of the line within the United States. And that's what we don't have here. I would like to 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: What provision are you 簫簫 what provision are you referring to?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: I'm pro 簫簫 referring to the first clause of the commercial activity exception, that it requires that the commercial activity be conducted, carried out in the United States. That means that within the territory of the United States. And I reached that conclusion by comparing it to the third clause, which refers to commercial activity outside of the United States having a direct effect in the United States, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Now if I may just say one point about agency. There is no dispute here that if the definition in the Act applies, RP doesn't fit within it. Now, this Court has recently held that we are guided by the language of the statute. I understand that arguments have been made by my colleagues and also by the Ninth Circuit that there is a difference between invocation of immunity and attribution. This is the point I want to make. Section 1604 is the invocation section. That's the section that states that certain people, those defined as the foreign state, can invoke immunity. Section 1605 is an attribution section. It says whose acts deprive you of immunity. And because foreign state is a defined term, we are limited to the limitations in the FSIA. In the alternative, if this Court were to move away from the definition, I believe we would all agree that there has to be some element of control. The test that this Court develops for agent has to have either the degree of control in Bancec or something lesser. But all agency law requires control, and that's where the en banc court missed the mark. They didn't require any control. And if one requires control, it has to be reversed because there was no control here. If there are no pending questions, I would appreciate reserving the balance of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Kneedler. SUPPORTING REVERSAL

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to start with the point about whether the FSIA simply incorporates due process standards, and we think it does not. It contains its own statutory terms which require interpretation. Congress did not simply incorporate the D.C. Long簫Arm Statute or due process principles or phrase the 簫簫 the statute in that way. It enacted specific statutory terms. And it's 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So is it the based簫upon language or the substantial簫context language?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: It's both. It's both and in the 簫簫 in the United States, which is an important point I want to make. The 簫簫 one of the reasons it's very different from just due process is because this 簫簫 the FSIA governs subject matter jurisdiction and personal jurisdiction to be sure, but based 簫簫 based upon a determination of immunity of a foreign state. And it does that by drawing, as was mentioned before, a strict territorial line that runs throughout all the exceptions. For example, the intentional 簫簫 the tort exception applies only to torts in the United States. And the property exception concerns property in the United States. And the focus of the commercial activity exception is also, at least under clause one 簫簫 well, all of them 簫簫 but clause one is commercial activity in the United States that also has a substantial connection, but the action has to be based upon, as Justice Scalia pointed out.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but how is based簫upon different from the language we routinely use in specific jurisdiction cases? In other words, it just seems as though Congress, in line with its objective of treating foreign government engaging in commercial activity in the same way as they would be treated in the foreign corporations case, in line with that objective, used language that's virtually synonymous with the language that we use in specific jurisdiction cases.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: But 簫簫 but it 簫簫 it did it in the context of a statutory structure that is designed to protect foreign sovereign immunity and not to draw U.S. courts into what could be very sensitive international questions of having U.S. courts pass judgment on what happens in a foreign country. For example 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, except it did so in the context of distinguishing between commercial activity and sovereign activity.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: In 簫簫 in Nelson 簫簫 actually, the pertinent paragraph in Nelson that discusses based簫upon, as counsel pointed out, the Court said you start with what is the conduct that 簫簫 that the suit is based upon. And the Court said the conduct there was the conduct that took place in Saudi Arabia. And then it had a discussion that said, to be sure, there was recruitment that 簫簫 that preceded that, and that recruitment put the employee in the position to be in the hospital in Saudi Arabia where the conduct occurred. But the focus was on, as the Court said, those torts in Saudi Arabia, not the 簫簫 not the antecedent commercial activity in the United States. That discussion in the Court's opinion preceded its discussion of the distinction between commercial activity and 簫簫 and sovereign activity. It had to do what 簫簫 with what is the focus of the claim. And there the Court was focusing on tort claims that happened outside the United States. And we think it's very important in this case to distinguish between tort claims and contract claims.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: What if you have a tort claim that has one element in the United States? How do 簫簫 how does a court determine whether the gravamen of the claim is in the United States or elsewhere?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: I think 簫簫 I think focusing on what 簫簫 on what the defendant's conduct is that actually caused the injury. And here, the 簫簫 the only difference between this case and Nelson is the argument that the purchase of the ticket, which is the 簫簫 by the way, the only commercial activity that was relied upon below. The Ninth Circuit says this at Phaneuf 13 and Footnote 4. Respondent is now trying to broaden that into the entire operations of the railroad which would actually be an assertion of general jurisdiction. Anything that would arise in the operation of a railroad in 簫簫 in Austria could be the subject of a suit if it was deemed to have substantial connection with the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: If we want to give based簫upon a separate substantive effect in this statute and so that it means something more substantial than mere due 簫簫 than what would suffice for due process, what 簫簫 what body of law, case law do we look to?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Well, I think in Nelson 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: If I want to find out about based簫on, what kind of cases am I supposed to read?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Well 簫簫 well 簫簫 and Nelson itself, I think, is very instructive on this because, first of all, the Court in Nelson did not 簫簫 did not describe what it was doing as simply applying due process standards. It was applying the statutory terms. And it 簫簫 again, it 簫簫 it focused on the particular conduct 簫簫 and this goes to Justice Alito's question as well 簫簫 it focused on the conduct that really caused the injury, which in that case was the 簫簫 were the intentional torts 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So Mr. 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: 簫簫 that happened in Saudi Arabia.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: In 簫簫 in my contract hypo, if the 簫簫 if the suit was a breach of contract action, do you think it would be based簫upon? Just a straight, like, you don't 簫簫 you didn't honor my ticket?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: On 簫簫 on a contract claim, I think that there well 簫簫 may well be a contract claim here. But I think it's important to recognize that a breach of contract claim, just because there is some connection with the United States, does not automatically 簫簫 even for due process purposes, some connection of a contract to the forum is not enough. The Court made that point in Burger King about contracts which was discussed in the 簫簫 in the Court's Walden v. Fiore case.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Kneedler, before you 簫簫 before you finish, you bring up an interest in relations with other nations. Do you know 簫簫 I mean, there are other nations that have similar legislation to the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act. Do we know how this case would come out, say, in Canada, Germany, or France under similar legislation?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: The European Convention provides for jurisdiction over tort claims, which this 簫簫 which this basically is, only with respect to torts that arise in the territory, which is 簫簫 which is the case here in our own tort exception, which is an important 簫簫 going back to Justice Kagan's question 簫簫 an important reason why this is different from due process. Tort claims under the FSIA, under 簫簫 under Exemption (5), Paragraph (a)(5), arise only when the tort occurs in the United States. And even business torts 簫簫 the House Report refers to business torts in the United States because there was a 簫簫 Congress was drawing a territorial line. And we 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The Netherlands 簫簫 the Netherlands and Switzerland have filed a brief, haven't they, in which they said that this injury, the claim that's pursued against here, the facts that underlie it, are based upon activity that took place in Switzerland.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: In Austria.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And so they wouldn't 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 under their law. Is there any indication 簫簫 I saw none in any of these briefs 簫簫 that there's any country in the world that would reach a different result?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: I 簫簫 as far as 簫簫 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: They've put lawyers on both sides, and I'm sure one of the lawyers would have told us if they'd found a country that would have reached 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: And this 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 result.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: The same general point is true in the UN convention on 簫簫 on immunity with respect to tort claims 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Kneedler, how about 簫簫 there is a contract claim in this lawsuit, so there is a 簫簫 is a kind of warranty of fitness kind of claim. How 簫簫 how about that? Why 簫簫 if you think that the 簫簫 let's assume that the contract claim, which is like you didn't honor my ticket, that there would be personal jurisdiction over, how do we separate out the warranty of habitability or fitness claim?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Because I think 簫簫 I think that's very parallel to what this Court confronted in the Nelson case where the 簫簫 the Court said the failure to warn 簫簫 there was a claim there too, a failure to warn about the hazard 簫簫 about the potential tort. And the 簫簫 and the court said, it 簫簫 it would not recognize jurisdiction by that sort of feint of language. You can't recharacterize something that is basically a tort abroad by claiming that there was a failure to warn 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Is 簫簫 is that a typical thing 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: 簫簫 about the tort. We think that's true here.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Is that a typical thing or an unusual thing to do in jurisdiction cases? In other words, to say, we're just not going to look at this claim by claim, we're going to ask about the whole gist of the lawsuit.

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: We think that, at least for tort claims, that, again, under our 簫簫 our position you look at the foundation of the claim, the gravamen of the claim. And applying that principle to tort claims, anyway, it's proper to look about where 簫簫 where the conduct occurred, the tortious 簫簫 the injury簫causing conduct occurred rather than the injury itself?

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Can the gravamen be in more than one place?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Is 簫簫 the based簫upon language, does it identify a single place, or could it be based簫upon activity in 簫簫 in two jurisdictions?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Well, it 簫簫 it could be based簫upon activity in two jurisdiction 簫簫 two jurisdictions. The House Report describing this talks about commercial activities occurring in whole or in part in the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But not in the airline case. You would not say that the airline case, the gravamen was the negligence in the United States and the injury when the plane landed?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: I think, depending on where the tort occurred, it may 簫簫 it may just be in the place where the 簫簫 where the negligence occurred. But I do want to say that for airlines 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It can't be both?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: Oh, it could be 簫簫 it could be because the question under the FSIA is whether you can sue in the United States. Whether you can sue abroad is not determined by based簫upon language in the 簫簫 in the FSIA. But the 簫簫 with respect to airlines, those 簫簫 those are covered by the Montreal Protocol which provides a variety of jurisdictions for suits to be brought 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, you're not suggesting the result be different if it were a boat, are you?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: No, I 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: No, but I 簫簫 but the hypotheticals, a lot of them, the transporter have to do with 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If this was a private company 簫簫

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: 簫簫 an airline.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: In your hypothetical.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 would there be jurisdiction here?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If this were a private company, not the state, would there be jurisdiction here?

turns.name: MR. KNEEDLER
turns.text: I think probably not, but I 簫簫 but I think it's 簫簫 I think it would be a good idea for the Court not to address the due process and just focus on the 簫簫 on the statutory terms here, because the question 簫簫 question of virtual presence by purchasing a ticket on the Internet can arise in all sorts of ways for due process purposes in private cases. And 簫簫 and here, we have a statute to focus on.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Kneedler. Mr. Fisher.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to start with Justice Sotomayor's question which I think has been asked a couple of other times today, which is, what would the answer be if this were a private defendant? And in footnote 11 of our brief on page 33, we explain why the answer would be that there would be specific jurisdiction. And in the Gibson簫Dunn amicus brief at pages 25 to 29 they give a fuller explanation. No response 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Why 簫簫 why, Mr. Fisher? There is one contact with the United States. A pass is bought from a travel agent in Massachusetts, a pass covering 30簫odd railroads. That's all that happened in the United States. All of the relevant conduct, the tortious conduct occurred abroad. I don't know of a single case where we have said specific jurisdiction can be based on a connection that had nothing to do with the injurious conduct.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: But, Justice Ginsburg, I think there are plenty of cases that support the proposition that when a company markets and sells a product in a jurisdiction, that creates specific jurisdiction. That's what OBB did through its agent, RPE, is market and sell its product.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: No, but not for everything. I mean, let's assume this. Let's assume that I get a brochure from the Vienna Opera Company, and I send back the order form, I'd like to buy tickets, okay? Now, for sure, if the Vienna Opera Company refused to honor my tickets, I have a claim, and it arises out of the fact that they have marketed my tickets in the United States. But now they honor my tickets, and I go up the stairs, and I slip in a puddle, and I injure myself. What does that have to do with the only contact that they've in the United States? I mean, at that point, you're just saying it's a but簫for test, and everything would be included. But I don't know of a sensible specific jurisdiction analysis that would 簫簫 that would run like that.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I agree with everything you said. But this case is different because our lawsuit is based on the duty of safe passage that gave rise in that sale 簫簫 in that ticket sale. So we're suing based on the promise and the offer 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: In the 簫簫 in the Austrian case of the Opera, there is an implied warranty that the Opera is fit for the purposes 簫簫 the opera house is fit for the purposes which it serves. Okay? Same thing. Not too hard to draft that.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I'm not sure. And if somebody did draft that, it would certainly be subject to a 12(b)(6) 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: What's the difference between saying that they have to warrant their product, which is an opera house, to be safe, to say that they have to warrant their trains safe for 簫簫 for passage? I can't see a difference. And it seems to me it's very normal in a contract to say that the seller of the product is implicitly warranting fitness. Isn't it?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, all I can say is in this case, we're suing on the exact warranty that gives rise from this ticket sale, Justice Breyer. And I think 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: That's 簫簫 that's the problem. I take it to go back to the questions you were asked, from your answer, you have found no case in any country where a waiver of sovereign immunity based on commercial activity supports you. And if so, what is that case? Switzerland and Britain 簫簫 or rather, Netherlands, say no. The EU Treaty says no. The UN Charter says no. And they have all these lists of language which, while not absolutely against you, leans against you. So I want to know what is there, in the law of sovereign immunity particularly, that argue 簫簫 leans in your favor.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: So, Justice Breyer, to 簫簫 let me be clear about what is and is not in that brief. There are no cases that I don't think any party has cited. What the 簫簫 what that brief says is that based on the language in various conventions, that this claim wouldn't be able to be brought somewhere else. But the language in those conventions are things like arise under, based upon, and so you basically end up in the same situation as you are here which is having to decide whether this claim can be brought. Remember that brief also alleges and it actually spends more time alleging that there is no proper principle簫agent relationship, which I think is 簫簫 the Court has probably realized is not a very credible argument. So I think you should be careful about putting too much weight on a brief that simply cites some treaty language. But I think the point I want to make, and this 簫簫 for the hypotheticals and to bring it back to my case, and I also tie in the contract 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: No. But I'm taking your answer to my question being we have none.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: We have none, and they have none. I have not found 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right. Okay. All right.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: You can ask him on rebuttal.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Could you tell me, your adversary claims you've been trying to change what your claim is. Is it based on the ticket sale? Or is it based on the operation of the train?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I think the better reading of the word "activity" in the statute for the reasons we describe in our brief is the overall integrated activity of running the railway train enterprise, which includes selling the product and delivering the product.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I see our interests, but I'm thinking there are 190簫some簫odd countries in the world. Many of them do have governments that run various kinds of enterprises. When they come to the United States, perhaps not being totally conversant with American law, they might think if my commercial activity in the United States really gives rise to some problem in the United States, I expect to be sued. But where what really gives rise to it 簫簫 you see, I have to use language like that 簫簫 takes place in my country, I expect it will be my courts that will deal with it.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, I don't know why any country would have thought that about this country's law, because before this 簫簫 you know, up until this point, the law in the lower courts, the D.C. Circuit and the Second Circuit, and most 簫簫 most specifically, have held that in this exact situation, there is jurisdiction.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, maybe 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: And Justice Breyer 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: 簫簫 they would've read 簫簫 if they had read our opinion in Nelson, that would have given them a good inclination to read sovereign immunity the way Justice Breyer suggests.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No. I'm glad you asked about Nelson, Mr. Chief Justice, because the paragraph in Nelson that Mr. Kneedler is referring to is distinguishing sovereign activity from commercial activity. The Court in Nelson did not have before it and was not concerned with the geographic nexus, and you know that because in that paragraph where it lists the things upon which the Nelsons' claim is not truly based, it lists not only the recruitment in the United States but the actual employment in Saudi Arabia.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, he has a paragraph in Nelson, one sentence of which helps you, that he says the Nelsons 簫簫 you know, they negotiated with the Nelsons in the United States. They entered into a contract in the United States. The Nelsons are heard aborad. I take it that's the basis of it, and this helps you. But he says before even taking each of the Nelsons' allegations about the recruitment and employment is true, those facts entitle the Nelsons to nothing under their theory of the case, which isn't contract. But, he goes on to say, it's these torts, which happened in the hospital abroad and not the arguably commercial activities that preceded their commission that formed the basis for the Nelsons' suit.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: So 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The basis for. You see. And so 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Justice Breyer 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 that's why I'm thinking it does not help you.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: When the Court said the arguable commercial activities that preceded it, they were talking not only about the recruitment in the United States but also the employment itself in Saudi Arabia. And they were distinguishing that from the police activity of seizing somebody and holding him in jail, which was what the case was really about which were sovereign activities. So the Court in Nelson 簫簫 Mr. Chief Justice, to be clear, I'm not saying Nelson supports me. I think Nelson simply doesn't answer this question because you were concerned in that case with sovereign versus commercial, not geographic nexus.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: So in line with the discussion we had earlier about specific jurisdiction, is your argument that general jurisdiction is enough to support based簫upon under the statute?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I thought you talked about just all of the different commercial activities that the Austrian airline engaged in in the United States. And if it's all of the different commercial activities, that sounds like general jurisdiction, not the specific jurisdiction.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No. I think OBB is subject to jurisdiction under the 簫簫 or 簫簫 I'm sorry, I should say does not have sovereign immunity under the FSIA for its train operation of selling tickets and getting people rides on the trains. It wouldn't be subject to jurisdiction if it had some 簫簫 if it had a mining enterprise on the side or something else that didn't even touch the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, suppose 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: So the activity 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Yes. But in point of fact, that's all OBB does. So, effectively, your argument is an argument that OBB is subject to jurisdiction in a general jurisdiction kind of way with respect to everything that they do. And, you know, I think what the Chief Justice is suggesting is, like, that seems wrong.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, let me say two things, Justice Kagan.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Thank you. I think that is the fairest reading of the statute, and Mr. Kneedler was talking about the House Report. One of the examples in the House Report of a commercial activity was running an airline. It's hard to distinguish running an airline from running a train system.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, suppose 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: So the terms of the statue do cover that. If 簫簫 Justice Alito, may I 簫簫 may I just say one more thing to Justice Kagan?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Sure. Finish your answer.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Which is if you didn't agree that the term "commercial activity" covered the entire enterprise, then you would ask the question whether the activity of selling tickets is enough to create a based簫upon argument in a specific jurisdiction since we went back to the conversation we've been having.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Suppose Ms. Sachs was Australian, and she bought this ticket on the Internet in Australia. Would you say that there would be no sovereign immunity in the suit in the United States?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, there wouldn't necessarily be sovereign 簫簫 there may not be sovereign immunity under the terms of the Act. But obviously, there would be a number of reasons why that case couldn't be brought 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: But there would be 簫簫 there would be 簫簫 there would not be immunity because it was based on the running of the railroad.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Right. Which 簫簫 which 簫簫 on the 簫簫 taking everything else the same, has substantial contact to the United States. But all the 簫簫 all we're talking about in this case is immunity. Section 1330, where it has jurisdiction over foreign states, has 簫簫 says that that jurisdiction has to be in personam. And obviously, that would carry with it the requirements for in personam jurisdiction which would require a contact in this country. And this is one thing I want to make sure, is the Court understands to bring us back to the hypotheticals we were talking about earlier and to tie in the contract hypothetical, it's important for the Court to understand there are all manner of cases where a duty is created in this country by way of a foreign state's commercial activities, and then injuries or breaches occur a broad. In fact, this is rather the oddball case. The more typical case is a high簫finance deal like the bonds case in the Weltover case, other kinds of international finance and loans. There are many employment cases where United States citizens sign an employment contract or are lured abroad, study abroad programs in the educational sphere, all kinds of situations where a duty is created in this country, but then all of the events that the lawsuit turns out to be about happen abroad.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, if 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: If that's the case 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Fisher, you 簫簫 you recognize 簫簫 I think you recognized in your brief that this 簫簫 you can call it a question of first impression. You 簫簫 you said, I believe, based on 簫簫 the meaning of based簫on in this context is an open question. And if it is an open question, why should we allow a foreign carrier to be sued in the United States for acts or omissions overseas 簫簫 the negligent conduct occurred overseas 簫簫 when the only link, the single link is a ticket, a pass, purchased from a 簫簫 a travel agent in the United States?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: For all the reasons I was just saying, Justice Ginsburg, because it is utterly common for duties to be created in this country 簫簫 here, the duty of safe passage and of utmost care 簫簫 and then the breach to occur a broad. My 簫簫 my friend on the other side was correct to say this case is indistinguishable from contract cases. There are 簫簫 there are all kinds of cases where duties are created in this country and then breached abroad.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But if that's true 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: The lower courts have always held that the FSIA 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: If that's true, I don't understand why you answered my hypothetical question the way you did, because in my hypothetical question I thought you said that there would not be jurisdiction to sue in the United States when I slip and fall in Vienna. And 簫簫 but you could make the same argument. Well, there was a duty created in the United States, and that's what I'm suing on.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I think when I answered your question I was imagining there wasn't a duty created. Maybe as Justice Breyer amended the hypothetical, there might be 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, there's 簫簫 I mean, the only thing that's happened in the United States is that I've purchased a ticket there. That's 簫簫 that's what I've done.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So I take it that your argument is that purchase of a ticket gives rise to a duty which is then violated when I slip and fall in another country.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I would say if the plaintiff alleges that in the lawsuit, then there would not be sovereign immunity. And then Justice Alito's 簫簫 I think as he was saying earlier, you would have a 簫簫 perhaps a very strong 12(b)(6) argument that there's no such State law claim that actually gave rise in that context.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: If 簫簫 if you get on the train in Vienna, and you buy your ticket in Vienna and you get on the train in Vienna, there is on the train operator some duty of care, isn't there? Without regard to 簫簫 in other words, there's nothing special about buying a ticket in the United States that gives rise to a unique duty of care that's any different from the duty of care that the railroad owes its passengers in Austria.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I think that's right, Mr. Chief Justice, but 簫簫 as long as you're talking about buying a ticket one place to the other. But of course that's true of any seller of any product that they 簫簫 that they stand 簫簫 you know, they offer the same thing depending on where you buy it. But the critical thing is where did they sell their ticket? They reached out to this country 簫簫 and it is worth answering your question with this important point, which is the product that they sold to Ms. Sachs is not available to Austrian citizens. This is a specially簫tailored product available only 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I understand. But there's nothing unique about the standard of care based upon whether it's a Eurail Pass or something else, is there? I'm just trying to suggest that you're putting an awful lot of weight on the standard of care created by the purchase of the ticket. And 簫簫 and that's no different here or whether the ticket was purchased in 簫簫 in Austria.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: It would 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: It makes no difference to what standard of care she would allege if there were some other basis for jurisdiction in the United States?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: That might be correct, but it is important not to lose sight of the fact that she didn't buy her ticket there. OBB reached out into this forum. And just going back to this Court's ordinary due process cases, when a business purposefully avails itself of the protections and the opportunities of a particular jurisdiction 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Fisher, the purposefully簫availed簫of test relates to specific jurisdiction, and there has to be an instant connecting. If there is that connection, then you must have, in addition, purposefully簫availed簫of. But purposefully簫availed簫of, standing alone, doesn't give you general jurisdiction, and it doesn't give you specific jurisdiction.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No. Fair enough. But you have to have a contact, and that's the substantial contact test in this 簫簫 in this case 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But you also have to have a relationship.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: And you have to have a relationship, which we do, because we purchased it here.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, let's say that I don't accept that argument, okay? Let's just for a moment assume that on a straight negligence claim, the idea that a negligence claim arising from an accident in Austria arises 簫簫 let's say I think it arises from the accident in Austria. It does not arise from the purchase of a ticket in the United States, okay?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Now, I guess there's still a question as to whether your warranty claims ought to be treated differently. And I take it what Mr. Kneedler has said about that is, no, they shouldn't be, that's just fancy pleading, it's just a way of converting a negligence claim into a contract claim. It's the same kind of thing that happened in Nelson that we refused to allow. Why isn't that right?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: It's not right because what you were worried about in Nelson is artful pleading to get around true sovereign immunity; in other words, to challenge sovereign acts dressed up as something else. Here there's no allegation that we're challenging sovereign acts. Everybody agrees we're challenging commercial acts. There's only the geographic nexus question. So we can plead a tort however we like subject to Rule 12(b)(6), which they can make on remand. And, Mr. Chief Justice, maybe I could turn back to one of yours questions, because I think it further highlights the difficulty with the other side's test. It has to be enough for a duty to create a cause of action, we say, because otherwise you're left with this gravamen test. And either 簫簫 gravamen test has to mean one of two things: Either it has to mean that only one particular place can be the gravamen, which is the way I understood it from their briefing. But as the Court, I think, is figuring out from this argument, you get into all heaps of troubles with contracts, employment, all kinds of other things where the injuries occur abroad, and how on earth is the Court to determine where the gravamen is. On the other hand, if gravamen is sort of a grava簫person's test so that you can have many multiple places where a claim can be brought, then I'm at a loss to understand how it's any improvement over the one element 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: How does it work with a domestic contract, an ordinary domestic contract entered into in Nevada, and the breach of the contract, which consisted of a failure, for example, to deliver goods to San Francisco, took place in California, and the lawsuit is brought in Nevada? How does that work? They move 簫簫 well, how does it happen?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Under due process, you'd have jurisdiction in Nevada.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I know that, but I mean where does the 簫簫 where does the 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: The gravamen?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Yeah, yeah. Does that not come up as to what law applies, as to a factor in forum non conveniens.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No, it 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: No, it doesn't? How do you decide what law applies? The contract doesn't say.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: The most 簫簫 there's a most significant relationship 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Ah. Most significant relationship. I see.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: 簫簫 every State, it says.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And does it matter whether it's 簫簫 is it to the formation of the contract or is it to the breach?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I think it's sometimes one and sometimes the other, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And what's the difference whether it's the one or the other?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: What's the difference?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: It depends 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I mean how does the Court decide?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: It depends 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I'm 簫簫 I'm simply pointing out that these kinds of problems are not unique to the international context.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No, I think 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: They arise in many different legal circumstances, and courts have to decide what is the gravamen, and sometimes that's difficult and sometimes it's not.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Am I right about that?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I've never seen this term used in the case law. And I think the other side was correct when they answered 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 the weight of the 簫簫 I don't know. You're the one 簫簫 I'm not very knowledgeable, and I 簫簫 I tend to believe you're more knowledgeable. Therefore, I'm asking you the question.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, all I can do is honestly answer that I've never seen this test used in any choice of law analysis.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: If we're dealing 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: I will add that 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: If we're dealing with choice of law, is 簫簫 is there any serious question of what law would govern the adequacy of OBB's boarding protocol, or their platform design? Would that be any law other than Austria?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, the Ninth Circuit held that California law applies in this case, and OBB has never 簫簫 has not challenged that holding. So as the case comes to this Court, California law applies for purposes of the one element 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And I'm 簫簫 I'm asking you. Never mind what the California court said. We have an accident in Austria, and it's based on 簫簫 the allegation is the boarding protocol was negligently designed, or the platform, the space between the train and the platform, negligently designed. What law would govern whether that platform was negligently designed, whether the boarding protocol, operating protocol was negligent?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: In all candor, Justice Ginsburg, there'd be a pretty good argument that Austria law should apply. And it is important for the Court to understand that simply holding that a suit under the FSIA can be brought in this country does not mean that U.S. law applies. There's a separate choice of law analysis that can be made. And so OBB had the opportunity to make choice of law arguments and has decided not to. But remember, they can do choice of law analysis not only in terms of ordinary common law principles that might apply in a court, but there's a number of ways that a defendant in OBB's position can protect itself in a case like this. First of all, they can put a choice of law provision in the ticket, or in the contract, or whatever might be at issue. Second of all, even as to forum, forum selection clauses can be put into tickets like this, and contracts, and in fact they are. As the United States told the Court at the cert stage of this case, all of these tickets now are governed by a forum selection clause provision, so you'll never see a case like this again, not only in the railroad context, but even in the airline industry.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And that 簫簫 and that works, even though it's an adhesion contract, and it's small print 簫簫

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Under Carnival Cruise Lines, this Court's decision, those kinds of forum selection clauses are perfectly enforceable.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: That 簫簫 that was an admiralty case, though.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, I don't know why it would be different in this context, Justice Kennedy. Certainly the government says that these are all governed by forum selection clauses now. We think they are, too. So what you're really deciding in this case, if I could end where perhaps I began, is you're not deciding transportation in this case, because all transportation cases are now covered by conventions and forum selection causes. What you're really deciding is what the law should be in the mainstream FSIA cases, like finance, like contracts. That's why Gibson簫Dunn has filed a case on behalf of a 簫簫 of a large hedge fund that says, we do business all the time with foreign sovereigns and we're very worried that if the Court adopts this gravamen based簫upon, we will not be able to enforce contracts and duties in this country that have given rise under negotiations in this country and then are breached abroad. Employment situations, like the hypothetical we give in our brief, where a United States citizen is hired as an engineer to go do oil and gas exploration and then something happens abroad. Those cases have always been brought in this country. And if this Court adopts the gravamen test, what you'll be saying is the courts now have some amorphous test that would seem, especially if there is only a single gravamen, that would seem to bounce all of these cases out of the United States courts, which would be dramatically 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, suppose 簫簫 suppose the Court simply said, buying a ticket from an agent in the United States is not enough, period. We don't adopt any gravamen. The question is, what does based簫on mean. And the Court could say based簫on is not based簫on if all that happened in the United States is the purchase of the ticket. We don't have to buy any gravamen or anything else.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Well, I think there is two reasons why you couldn't just limit it to that, Justice Ginsburg. First, as a legal matter, our claim depends on the duty that arose when that ticket's purchase was made. And I don't see any legal way to distinguish duties that arose in a ticket sale from other kinds of duties that arise and all kinds of other marketing and contract settings. And second of all, just as a matter of common sense, remember 簫簫 let's go back to where we began 簫簫 this is a commercial activity exception. If they weren't selling tickets, it wouldn't even be commercial to begin with. So it would be a highly odd holding to say that the one thing that makes this commercial is what prevents it from being brought in the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: What makes it commercial is it's a railroad doing the same thing a private railroad would do. It's in 簫簫 its business is operating a railroad.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: That's right, Justice Ginsburg. And if you want us to ask the question in terms of whether a private company would be subject to jurisdiction under the same settings here, at least all you have to hold is that OBB should be in the same shoes as a private company. Now, we say in our brief and in the Gibson brief in more detail, why due process wouldn't stand in the way of jurisdiction there. But if you have any doubt about that, you can note that OBB has made a personal jurisdiction argument in the district court that the district court never reached. And so that is available on remand. The only thing before this Court is sovereign immunity and whether OBB is entitled to sovereign immunity for commercial acts in this country and the teeth of a congressional statute that says in the declaration of purpose in Section 1602 that foreign states doing commercial activities in this country should not be entitled to sovereign immunity. And then in Section 1606 says: What we want to do is 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And we say, look, did 簫簫 they're 簫簫 your exact words.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Are they liable for activity that took place in this country? They are liable for the breach of the contract. The breach of the contract took place in Austria.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: End of case.

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: MR. FISHER
turns.text: Because 簫簫 because what the definition of in the United States, Justice Breyer, and this is sub (D) of 1603(c), says that 簫簫 I'm sorry, it's sub 簫簫 sub (E) of 1603(c) says that "in the United States" is defined as substantial contact with the United States. And so that can occur in whole or in part in this country, and in part 簫簫 I'm sorry, in part includes the ticket sale.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Basombrio, you have three minutes remaining.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Much of our discussion today has been in separating the torts from the contractual claims here in this case, implied warranty claims. Let me tell you why I believe it's a bad idea to split claims on causes of action. This Court decided recently a case called Pimentel in which the Republic of the Philippines had sovereign immunity. But there were also a number of other defendants that did not enjoy sovereign immunity. And this Court, taking into consideration important issues such as international comity, decided that under Rule 19, the entire lawsuit had to be dismissed, including as to nonsovereign entities, in order to give full effect to the sovereign immunity of the Republic of the Philippines. In light of that holding, it would make little sense that when the defendant has the right to invoke immunity, that we would split the causes of action so that some of them would proceed in Austria and some of them would proceed in the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Are you suggesting the based簫upon determination is not done on a claim簫by簫claim basis, it's 簫簫 you look at the entire complaint?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: That's why 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: You find the gravamen of the entire complaint?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Yes, exactly, Your Honor. And the circuit courts and the State courts have all understood what gravamen means. They know what it means. And if we just took anybody off the street here and we asked them, why is the Respondent suing, each person would say, because she was injured in a terrible accident in Austria.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, what if the only claim were the breach of warranty claim?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Then we would have to ask where did the breach occur. As in Justice Kagan's question, I would answer, if the breach occurred in Austria, then the claim would arise in Austria. Now, the warranty, in terms of the duty of care, there is no duty of care until she arrives at the station and tries to board that train. And even if she didn't have a ticket at all, the record establishes that we would still owe her a duty of care.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: What if she also had, in addition to the claims she's asserted, a conventional breach of contract claim? She said that when they 簫簫 when she tried to get on the train, they said no, your Eurail Pass doesn't let you get on free, you have to pay an additional amount?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: That would have said 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: We look to the gravamen of the whole thing and we say, well, the tort claims are in Austria and therefore she can't bring this breach of contract claim in the United States?

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Well, if she had been 簫簫 if she had been hurt and in addition to that, they would have told her 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, exactly what happened here, plus she said they made me pay extra.

turns.name: MR. BASOMBRIO
turns.text: Then the gravamen would still be Austria because that's where all those events took place.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
